source_paper_title: On the Optimality of Greedy Policies in Dynamic Matching
context:
Page 17 / 23, Remark 6.2 (Section 6, near end)

Whether—or more specifically, under which conditions—the best achievable regret grows with n remains an open question.
title: Determine whether minimal all-time regret scales with number of agent types
background_latex:
Consider a centralized discrete-time dynamic matching market with a finite set of agent types \(A=\{1,\dots,n\}\). In each period \(t=1,2,\dots\), exactly one agent arrives; its type \(i\in A\) is drawn i.i.d. with probabilities \(\lambda_i>0\) and \(\sum_{i\in A}\lambda_i=1\). Each type \(i\) has an associated queue holding waiting agents of that type.

There is a set of feasible two-way matches \(M\). Each match \(m\in M\) connects exactly two types \(A(m)=\{i,j\}\subseteq A\) and yields value \(r_m>0\) when executed. Executing \(m\) consumes one agent from each of the two endpoint queues and the matched agents depart immediately.

A (nonanticipative) matching policy \(D\) decides after each arrival which feasible match (if any) to execute. Let \(D^t_m\) be the cumulative number of times match \(m\) has been executed by time \(t\), and let the expected total value accrued by time \(t\) be
\[
R_{D,t}:=\mathbb{E}[r\cdot D^t]=\mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{m\in M} r_m D^t_m\Big].
\]
Define the hindsight (offline) benchmark at time \(t\) as
\[
R^*_t := \mathbb{E}\Big[\max\{r\cdot y : My \le A^t,\ y\in \mathbb{Z}^d_{\ge 0}\}\Big],
\]
where \(A^t\in\mathbb{Z}^n_{\ge 0}\) is the random vector of cumulative arrivals by time \(t\), \(d:=|M|\), and \(M\in\{0,1\}^{n\times d}\) is the matching-incidence matrix with \(M_{im}=1\) if and only if type \(i\in A(m)\). The all-time regret of policy \(D\) at time \(t\) is \(R^*_t-R_{D,t}\).

A policy is called hindsight optimal if its regret is uniformly bounded over time, i.e., \(R^*_t-R_{D,t}=O(1)\) as \(t\) varies.

In the setting studied in the paper (two-way matching networks satisfying a suitable “general position” condition), it is known that some greedy policies achieve regret bounded by a constant that, in the proved upper bound, grows linearly with the number of agent types \(n\).
problem_definition_latex:
Let \(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) denote the minimal achievable all-time regret level for a given two-way matching network instance \(G=(M,\lambda,r)\), defined by
\[
\mathcal{R}^*(G):=\inf_{D\in\Pi}\ \sup_{t>0}\bigl(R^*_t-R_{D,t}\bigr),
\]
where \(\Pi\) is the class of admissible (nonanticipative) matching policies.

Determine whether—or specify conditions under which—\(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) must grow as a function of the number of agent types \(n\).
latex:
Consider a centralized discrete-time dynamic matching market with a finite set of agent types \(A=\{1,\dots,n\}\). In each period \(t=1,2,\dots\), exactly one agent arrives; its type \(i\in A\) is drawn i.i.d. with probabilities \(\lambda_i>0\) and \(\sum_{i\in A}\lambda_i=1\). Each type \(i\) has an associated queue holding waiting agents of that type.

There is a set of feasible two-way matches \(M\). Each match \(m\in M\) connects exactly two types \(A(m)=\{i,j\}\subseteq A\) and yields value \(r_m>0\) when executed. Executing \(m\) consumes one agent from each of the two endpoint queues and the matched agents depart immediately.

A (nonanticipative) matching policy \(D\) decides after each arrival which feasible match (if any) to execute. Let \(D^t_m\) be the cumulative number of times match \(m\) has been executed by time \(t\), and let the expected total value accrued by time \(t\) be
\[
R_{D,t}:=\mathbb{E}[r\cdot D^t]=\mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{m\in M} r_m D^t_m\Big].
\]
Define the hindsight (offline) benchmark at time \(t\) as
\[
R^*_t := \mathbb{E}\Big[\max\{r\cdot y : My \le A^t,\ y\in \mathbb{Z}^d_{\ge 0}\}\Big],
\]
where \(A^t\in\mathbb{Z}^n_{\ge 0}\) is the random vector of cumulative arrivals by time \(t\), \(d:=|M|\), and \(M\in\{0,1\}^{n\times d}\) is the matching-incidence matrix with \(M_{im}=1\) if and only if type \(i\in A(m)\). The all-time regret of policy \(D\) at time \(t\) is \(R^*_t-R_{D,t}\).

A policy is called hindsight optimal if its regret is uniformly bounded over time, i.e., \(R^*_t-R_{D,t}=O(1)\) as \(t\) varies.

In the setting studied in the paper (two-way matching networks satisfying a suitable “general position” condition), it is known that some greedy policies achieve regret bounded by a constant that, in the proved upper bound, grows linearly with the number of agent types \(n\).

Let \(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) denote the minimal achievable all-time regret level for a given two-way matching network instance \(G=(M,\lambda,r)\), defined by
\[
\mathcal{R}^*(G):=\inf_{D\in\Pi}\ \sup_{t>0}\bigl(R^*_t-R_{D,t}\bigr),
\]
where \(\Pi\) is the class of admissible (nonanticipative) matching policies.

Determine whether—or specify conditions under which—\(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) must grow as a function of the number of agent types \(n\).
open_problem_quote: Whether—or more specifically, under which conditions—the best achievable regret grows with n remains an open question.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over small networks (graphs), arrival vectors, and match values and approximate \(\inf_D \sup_t\) regret by restricting to a parametric family of policies; however, optimizing over all admissible policies is not directly tractable, so automated search can at best provide evidence or lower bounds on restricted classes.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (policies within a chosen representable class, e.g., priority rules or max-weight variants) are programmatically specifiable and can be evaluated by simulation to estimate \(\sup_{t\le T}\) regret on sampled instances. This can help discover instances suggesting \(n\)-dependence or policies with smaller apparent \(n\)-growth, but the fitness signal is noisy and does not certify optimality over all policies.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Kerimov–Ashlagi–Gurvich (2023) show that in two-way matching networks satisfying the general position (GP) condition (unique nondegenerate optimum of the static-planning LP (SPP)), there exist greedy policies that are hindsight optimal, i.e., achieve \(\sup_t (R_t^*-R_{D,t})<\infty\). Their analysis ties regret to queue-length control on the over-demanded set \(Q_0\) and uses the residual graph \(G'\) obtained by removing redundant edges \(M_0\). For the longest-queue greedy policy \(\mathrm{LQ}(M_+)\), they obtain an explicit uniform regret bound \(R_t^*-R_{D,t}\le r_{\max} n/\varepsilon\) (for large enough \(t\)), where \(\varepsilon\) is the GP gap. This is the origin of the open question: is the linear dependence on the number of types \(n\) an artifact of analysis, or is growth in \(n\) unavoidable for \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) on some families of instances?

Forward-citing work provides additional constant-regret constructions and analytical frameworks but, based on the provided summaries, does not yet settle the \(n\)-scaling question. "Achieving constant regret for dynamic matching via state-independent policies" appears to broaden sufficient conditions and simplify policy structure (state-independent decisions), which could potentially yield constants with improved dependence on network size, but the available metadata does not state whether the regret constant is independent of \(n\) or provides lower bounds. "Constant regret primal-dual policy for multi-way dynamic matching" suggests that primal-dual/dual-potential methods can deliver uniform regret even with hyperedge matches; this is conceptually aligned with the dual/surplus-vector viewpoint in the (SPP) analysis, but again the dependence on \(n\) is unclear. "Batching and Greedy Policies: How Good Are They in Dynamic Matching?" may provide separations or bounds for natural policy classes that could imply \(n\)-dependent performance, but the abstract snippet does not specify an all-time regret metric.

Related-tool papers indicate promising directions for resolving the open question: polyhedral/achievability characterizations ("Online Stochastic Matching: A Polytope Perspective") may connect regret constants to geometric margins of the matching-rate polytope, while LP-based regret analyses in revenue management show explicit linear-in-dimension constants under nondegeneracy, suggesting that dimension dependence can be intrinsic in online LP control. At present, however, no cited work in the provided set establishes a lower bound forcing \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) to grow with \(n\), nor a theorem giving \(n\)-uniform constants under broad conditions. Thus the problem remains open: identifying instance families where \(\mathcal R^*(G)\ge \Omega(f(n))\) for some growing \(f\), or conversely proving \(\mathcal R^*(G)=O(\varepsilon^{-1})\) with constants independent of \(n\) under additional structural restrictions on \(G'\) (e.g., bounded degree, bounded treewidth, or uniformly bounded number/size of residual components).

sources.title: Achieving constant regret for dynamic matching via state-independent policies
sources.theorem_or_result:
From the title/abstract only: the paper claims existence of “state-independent” (i.e., not depending on current queue lengths) online matching policies for stochastic two-way dynamic matching networks that achieve constant (uniform-in-time) regret relative to the offline/hindsight benchmark, under some structural conditions on the network/parameters. Precisely, it appears to establish 
- There exists a policy class \,\(D\) with decisions based only on primitives \((M,\lambda,r)\) (not the state) such that \(\sup_{t\ge 1}(R_t^*-R_{D,t})\le C(M,\lambda,r)\) for a finite constant \(C\).
The abstract snippet does not specify how \(C\) scales with the number of types \(n\), nor whether lower bounds are proved.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether the minimal achievable all-time regret level \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) must grow with the number of types \(n\), or under what conditions it does. This citing paper is directly about achieving constant regret (uniformly bounded regret) and, by emphasizing “state-independent policies,” may provide improved upper bounds and/or conditions guaranteeing \(\mathcal R^*(G)=O(1)\) for broad classes of networks.

However, based only on title/abstract, it is unclear whether the paper:
- proves that the constant regret bound can be chosen independent of \(n\) (which would strongly answer the question in the negative for those instances),
- or instead provides a constant bound \(C\) that still scales with \(n\) (e.g., linearly),
- or provides any lower bounds showing necessary growth of \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) with \(n\).

Thus, it appears to contribute upper-bound/achievability results (and possibly broader sufficient conditions) but does not, from available information, resolve the scaling question for \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) in general.

sources.title: Constant regret primal-dual policy for multi-way dynamic matching
sources.theorem_or_result:
From the title/abstract only: the paper proposes a primal-dual online policy for discrete-time stochastic dynamic matching with multi-way (hyperedge) matches and claims “constant regret” (uniformly bounded over time) relative to an offline benchmark, i.e., 
- There exists a primal-dual policy with 
\[\sup_{t\ge 1}\bigl(R_t^*-R_{D,t}\bigr)=O(1).\]
The abstract does not specify how the constant depends on the number of types \(n\), the network size, or other parameters, nor the precise assumptions (e.g., general position, nondegeneracy, arrival rates).

sources.title: Batching and Greedy Policies: How Good Are They in Dynamic Matching?
sources.theorem_or_result: From the title/abstract only, the paper appears to analyze performance guarantees for greedy and batching policies in a dynamic nonbipartite stochastic matching model with type-specific arrival distributions and waiting queues. No specific theorem, regret definition, or scaling result in the number of types \(n\) is stated in the provided abstract, so a precise key theorem cannot be extracted.
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether the minimal achievable all-time (uniform-in-time) regret \(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) must grow with the number of types \(n\). This citing paper is plausibly directly related because it studies the same general class of dynamic (nonbipartite) stochastic matching problems and explicitly compares greedy policies (which are known to have \(O(1)\) regret under general-position assumptions) and batching policies. If the paper proves lower bounds on the regret of greedy/batching policies, or instance-dependent bounds that scale with network size/number of types, it could provide partial progress toward showing that \(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\) (or at least the regret of natural policy classes) must grow with \(n\). However, based on the abstract snippet alone, it is unclear whether the paper addresses uniform-in-time regret, the offline benchmark \(R_t^*\), or any \(n\)-scaling lower bound for \(\mathcal{R}^*(G)\).

sources.title: Online Stochastic Matching: A Polytope Perspective
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper develops a polytope/conservation-law framework for two-way stochastic matching on a compatibility graph with Poisson/i.i.d. arrivals. sources.title: An improved analysis of LP-based control for revenue management
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies i.i.d. online revenue management / online LP with finite support and unknown arrival probabilities. For the classic LP-based adaptive (frequent re-solving) algorithm, proves: - If the certainty-equivalent LP is unique and nondegenerate, regret versus the fluid benchmark is uniformly bounded in horizon length: \(\mathrm{Reg}_T^\pi \le (48m+4n+12)\,\|\lambda^*\|_1/L^2 + o(1)\) (Theorem 2), where \(m\) is #resources, \(n\) is #order types, \(\lambda^*\) is an optimal dual price vector, and \(L\) is an LP stability margin. - Without nondegeneracy, regret is \(O((m\sqrt n+n)\sqrt T\log T)\) (Theorem 3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether the minimal achievable all-time regret \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) in dynamic two-way matching must grow with the number of agent types \(n\). This paper does not analyze dynamic matching regret or \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) directly; it analyzes a different but related i.i.d. online LP/revenue-management model with accept/reject decisions and resource budgets, and measures regret against the fluid (certainty-equivalent) LP (and discusses hindsight bounds in related work). Its bounded-regret result in the nondegenerate case has an explicit linear dependence on the number of order types \(n\) (and resources \(m\)), via \((48m+4n+12)\|\lambda^*\|_1/L^2\). This supports the plausibility that constant-in-time regret bounds can scale at least linearly with the number of types in LP-based formulations, but it neither proves necessity of such scaling nor addresses the matching-network structure or the minimal regret \(\mathcal R^*(G)\). The main relevance is methodological: LP stability/nondegeneracy margins and constraint-process martingale analysis that could be adapted to study how regret constants depend on network size/types in dynamic matching.

sources.title: Matching impatient and heterogeneous demand and supply
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a high-volume (fluid) approximation for stochastic bipartite matching with heterogeneous demand/supply, abandonment (patience times), and holding costs; derives an optimization problem whose optimal solution gives asymptotically optimal matching rates on fluid scale, and proposes policies (discrete-review and, under an extreme-point condition such as increasing hazard rate patience distributions, a state-independent edge-priority policy) that asymptotically achieve those rates.
sources.connection: The open problem concerns whether the minimal achievable uniform-in-time (all-time) regret \,\(\mathcal R^*(G)\), relative to an offline benchmark, must grow with the number of types \(n\) in a discrete-time two-way matching model without abandonment/holding costs. This citing paper studies a different objective/regime: long-run average value minus waiting costs in a continuous-time/high-volume setting with reneging, and proves fluid-scale asymptotic optimality of certain policies. It does not analyze all-time regret, uniform-in-time bounds, or how regret constants scale with \(n\). Indirectly, it provides techniques (fluid invariants, extreme-point/priority structure) that could inform policy design or scaling arguments in larger networks, but it does not resolve whether \(\mathcal R^*(G)\) must grow with \(n\) in the original regret framework.
